name: Fleabag
description: Single-camera BBC dramedy (2016, 2019) created by and starring Phoebe Waller-Bridge as a sharp, grief-stricken, fourth-wall-breaking Londoner navigating sex, family and faith. No laugh track; acerbic, confessional, direct-address comedy. Scored as-is.

name: Chappelle's Show
description: Sketch comedy series (Comedy Central, 2003-2006). Dave Chappelle's incendiary racial and pop-culture satire, anchored by his stand-up wraparounds before a live audience. Iconic recurring bits and one-off characters: Clayton Bigsby, Tyrone Biggums, the Rick James and Prince true-Hollywood-stories, Black Bush, the Racial Draft. Sketch format scored as-is; jokes attributed to the on-screen character. Includes the 3-episode 'Lost Episodes' (S3); the 'Greatest Misses' clip episode is excluded.

name: Veep
description: Single-camera HBO political satire from Armando Iannucci. Selina Meyer's struggle through the Vice Presidency and beyond. Walk-and-talk patter, profanity-laced insult comedy, and cringe escalation. Dialogue-dense by design — every joke is on the page.

name: Seinfeld
description: Multi-camera sitcom about 'nothing.' Dense observational and absurdist humor. Complex plot structures where multiple storylines converge. Known for neologisms and cultural catchphrases.

name: Flight of the Conchords
description: Single-camera musical comedy. Two-man New Zealand folk-parody band (Bret McKenzie, Jemaine Clement) struggling in NYC. Distinctive form: each episode is built around 1-2 original songs that function as comedic set pieces. Dialogue is dry, observational, deadpan. Songs scored as jokes per the 2026-06-01 musical-comedy methodology rule.

name: Broad City
description: Single-camera Comedy Central buddy comedy (2014-2019). Abbi Jacobson and Ilana Glazer as two broke twentysomething women turning everyday NYC errands into absurd misadventures. No laugh track; raunchy, improv-inflected, friendship-driven. Scored as-is.

name: 30 Rock
description: Single-camera workplace comedy. Extremely high joke density — rapid-fire punchlines, cutaway gags, and wordplay. Absurdist and meta-referential. One of the highest JPM shows in television.

name: Arrested Development
description: Single-camera comedy with narrator. Extremely dense callbacks, visual gags, and layered wordplay. Rewards rewatching. Known for foreshadowing and running gags that span seasons.

name: Curb Your Enthusiasm
description: Single-camera HBO improv comedy. Larry David turns social minutiae into escalating disaster. No laugh track; cringe and callback structure.

name: Freaks and Geeks
description: Single-camera dramedy (NBC, 1999–2000). Paul Feig and Judd Apatow's one-season cult classic set at a 1980 Michigan high school — Lindsay Weir drifting into the burnout "freaks" while her brother Sam navigates the "geeks." Naturalistic, character-first comedy: awkward-teen cringe, deadpan, and grounded observation over joke volume. No laugh track.

name: The Office
description: Single-camera mockumentary workplace comedy. Primarily cringe/discomfort humor with character-driven jokes. Tone ranges from subtle observational to broad physical comedy. Known for talking-head confessionals and Jim's camera looks.

name: It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia
description: Single-camera dark comedy about terrible people. Dark/subversive humor, cringe comedy, and escalating absurdity. Characters are intentionally awful, which is the joke.

name: The Simpsons
description: Animated family sitcom — the full classic run (S1–S20). Dense satire, character comedy, and rapid cultural reference. Note: subtitle-based scoring captures dialogue but under-counts the show’s heavy visual/sight gags (chalkboard, couch gags, background signs).

name: Community
description: Single-camera ensemble comedy set at a community college study group. Highly meta and genre-parodic — concept episodes (paintball wars, alternate timelines, claymation, bottle episodes) that subvert sitcom form. Reference-dense, character-driven, with escalating absurdity. Craft-forward rather than joke-volume-forward.

name: Parks and Recreation
description: Single-camera mockumentary workplace comedy set in local government. Warm character-driven humor with absurdist elements. Talking-head confessionals similar to The Office but generally more optimistic.

name: Futurama
description: Animated sci-fi sitcom from the Simpsons creators. Scored S1-S10 incl. Hulu revival.

name: Taxi
description: Louie De Palma is a cantankerous, acerbic taxi dispatcher in New York City. He tries to maintain order over a collection of varied and strange characters who drive for him. As he bullies and insults them from the safety of his “cage,” they form a special bond among themselves, becoming friends and s

name: Schitt's Creek
description: Single-camera comedy about a wealthy family forced to live in a small town. Character comedy driven by fish-out-of-water situations. Warm humor that deepens as characters grow.

name: The Larry Sanders Show
description: Single-camera HBO showbiz satire. Garry Shandling stars as Larry Sanders, the paranoid, insecure host of a late-night talk show; the series alternates between mock on-air segments and dry, observational behind-the-scenes interplay with Hank Kingsley (Jeffrey Tambor) and Artie (Rip Torn). No laugh track, no audience — the show that taught HBO comedy how to feel real. Proto-Office, proto-30 Rock, proto-Curb. Heavy on character craft, awkward-pause cringe, and industry-insider callbacks.

name: The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air
description: Multi-camera NBC sitcom with a live audience and laugh track. Street-smart West Philadelphia teen Will (Will Smith) is sent to live with his wealthy aunt and uncle in Bel-Air. Fast, brash joke delivery, physical comedy, fourth-wall winks, and Carlton's earnestness as foil; tonal range from broad farce to landmark dramatic episodes. Note: famous mid-run recast of Aunt Viv (Janet Hubert S1-3, Daphne Maxwell Reid S4-6).

name: Friends
description: Multi-camera sitcom about six friends in New York. Mix of relationship humor, physical comedy, and catchphrase-driven jokes. Broader comedy style aimed at wide audiences.

name: Brooklyn Nine-Nine
description: Single-camera workplace comedy set in a police precinct. Mix of character comedy, physical humor, and cold open gags. Ensemble cast with distinct comedic voices.

name: The Big Bang Theory
description: Multi-camera sitcom about physicists and their social struggles. Reference-heavy humor, catchphrases, and nerd-culture jokes. High joke volume, broad comedic style.

name: Two and a Half Men
description: Multi-camera sitcom originally centered on a hedonistic jingle writer, his uptight brother, and his nephew. Later seasons replaced the lead. Relies heavily on sexual innuendo, insult comedy, and broad physical humor. High joke volume, laugh-track-heavy format.

name: All in the Family
description: Archie Bunker, a working class bigot, constantly squabbles with his family over the important issues of the day.

name: The Jeffersons
description: Sitcom following a successful African-American couple, George and Louise “Weezyö Jefferson as they “move on up” from working-class Queens to a ritzy Manhattan apartment. A spin-off of All in the Family.

name: M*A*S*H
description: The 4077th Mobile Army Surgical Hospital is stuck in the middle of the Korean war. With little help from the circumstances they find themselves in, they are forced to make their own fun. Fond of practical jokes and revenge, the doctors, nurses, administrators, and soldiers often find ways of making

name: The Mary Tyler Moore Show
description: 30-year-old single Mary Richards moves to Minneapolis to start a new life after a romantic break-up. There she reacquaints with Phyllis who rents her a room, and meets her upstairs neighbor and new best friend Rhoda. Mary unexpectedly lands a job as associate producer at the TV station WJM, where sh

name: WKRP in Cincinnati
description: When a Cincinnati radio station switches from sedate music to top-40 rock 'n' roll, its staff of oddball characters is forced to switch gears quickly. New programming director Andy Travis brings in a new DJ named Venus Flytrap to work with the station's burned-out veteran, Dr. Johnny Fever. Neurotic

name: Sanford and Son
description: The misadventures of a cantankerous junk dealer and his frustrated son.

name: Barney Miller
description: Barney Miller is the kind of cop we'd all like to run into. Always sensible, he maintains order over a band of detectives who gamble, hit on anything in skirts, go to renaissance philosophy conventions for fun, and would really prefer to be writing. Nearly all of the action takes place in the squad

name: Cheers
description: Multi-camera bar sitcom filmed before a live audience. Regulars at a Boston bar trade barbs; Sam and Diane's will-they-won't-they. Sharp ensemble banter and running gags.

name: Frasier
description: Multi-camera Cheers spin-off. Pompous radio psychiatrist Frasier Crane in Seattle. Farce, verbal wit, and highbrow/lowbrow class comedy.

name: How I Met Your Mother
description: Multi-camera sitcom told in flashback about a NYC friend group. Barney's catchphrases and elaborate bits over serialized romantic arcs.

name: Modern Family
description: Single-camera mockumentary about three connected families. Talking-head confessionals, warm character comedy, and farcical plots.

name: Scrubs
description: Single-camera hospital comedy with fantasy cutaways and narration. Mixes broad slapstick with sincere drama. No laugh track.

name: The Golden Girls
description: Multi-camera sitcom about four older women sharing a Miami home. Filmed before a live audience; quick insults, innuendo, and warmth.

name: Roseanne
description: Multi-camera working-class family sitcom filmed before a live audience. Blue-collar realism, sarcasm, and domestic chaos.

name: Everybody Loves Raymond
description: Multi-camera family sitcom filmed before a live audience. Observational marriage-and-in-laws comedy built on relatable friction.

name: Night Court
description: Multi-camera sitcom set in a Manhattan night court. Filmed before a live audience; rapid one-liners and absurd defendants.

name: I Love Lucy
description: Pioneering multi-camera sitcom filmed before a live audience. Lucy's schemes and physical comedy set the template for the genre.

name: The Dick Van Dyke Show
description: Multi-camera sitcom split between a TV writers' room and suburban home life. Filmed before a live audience; elegant verbal and physical comedy.

name: Newhart
description: Multi-camera sitcom with Bob Newhart running a Vermont inn among eccentric locals. Dry, reactive deadpan before a live audience.

name: Will & Grace
description: Multi-camera sitcom about a gay lawyer and his straight best friend. Filmed before a live audience; rapid quips and broad farce.

name: Malcolm in the Middle
description: Single-camera family sitcom from a gifted kid's POV. No laugh track; frenetic physical comedy and fourth-wall asides.

name: Happy Days
description: Multi-camera nostalgia sitcom set in 1950s-60s Milwaukee. Fonzie's cool, family warmth, and catchphrase comedy.
